ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Rakhine Mont Ti Thoke. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Rakhine Mont Ti Thoke specifically. A0CKP (Noodles, cooked) is used as the closest defensible parent. The dish is a dressed/salad preparation of rice noodles with fish sauce, chickpea flour, and aromatics.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific Burmese regional noodle salad dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Rakhine Mont Ti Thoke is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous serving of dressed rice noodles with condiments and toppings as typically served in a restaurant setting.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: rice noodles (cooked) and Burmese noodle salad dressing components; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Rakhine Mont Ti Thoke exists in any approved primary database. Values are estimated from a weighted component analysis: approximately 65% cooked rice noodles, 10% chickpea flour preparation, 10% fish sauce and ngapi dressing, 5% dried shrimp, 10% aromatics (shallots, garlic, chilli, coriander, lime). FAO/INFOODS SEA values used for rice noodles and fish sauce; USDA SR Legacy used for chickpea flour, dried shrimp, and aromatics. Energy calculated by Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles, cooked — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to Rakhine Mont Ti Thoke as a rice-noodle-dominant dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Rakhine Mont Ti Thoke. The GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles (GI approximately 53, low category). The dish is predominantly rice noodles (~65% by weight); the acidic dressing (lime juice, fish sauce) and protein/fat components (dried shrimp, chickpea flour) are expected to moderately lower the effective GI relative to plain rice noodles. GI 53 is therefore a conservative upper-bound estimate. GL per serving = (53 × 24.5 × 300/100) / 100 = 39.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and fermented fish paste (ngapi)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Rakhine Mont Ti Thoke due to its high sodium content (approximately 520 mg per 100 g, equating to ~1560 mg per standard 300 g bowl), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced fish sauce and ngapi at the restaurant is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodle base, partially offset by acidic dressing and protein components
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Rakhine Mont Ti Thoke in controlled portions, as the rice noodle base contributes a moderate-to-high glycemic load per serving (~39 GL units); pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and limiting portion size to half a standard bowl can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from fish-based condiments; partially offset by low saturated fat in rice noodle base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious with Rakhine Mont Ti Thoke due to its high sodium content and moderate cholesterol contribution from dried shrimp and fish paste, and should limit consumption to occasional servings while requesting reduced condiment quantities.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rakhine Mont Ti Thoke (ရခိုင်မုန့်တီသုတ်) is a traditional cold noodle salad originating from Rakhine State (also known as Arakan) in western Myanmar. The dish features thin rice noodles (mont ti) tossed with a pungent, umami-rich dressing made from fish sauce, fermented fish paste (ngapi), dried shrimp, chickpea flour, lime juice, garlic oil, shallots, and fresh chilli. The noodles are typically served at room temperature or slightly chilled, giving the dish a refreshing quality despite its bold, savoury flavour profile. The texture combines the soft, slightly chewy rice noodles with the crunch of fried shallots and the granular richness of chickpea flour. Rakhine Mont Ti Thoke is a staple street food and home dish in Rakhine State and has gained popularity across Myanmar and in Burmese diaspora communities in Singapore and other Southeast Asian cities. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~148 kcal per 100 g), with a carbohydrate-dominant macronutrient profile from the rice noodle base, modest protein from dried shrimp and fish paste, and low fat. The sodium content is notably high due to the fish sauce and ngapi, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low category) based on the rice noodle base, though the glycemic load per standard serving is moderate to high. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish or shellfish allergies.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Rakhine Mont Ti Thoke exists in any approved primary nutrient database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA). All nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component analysis of the dish's known ingredients using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy data for individual components. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles. Confidence is therefore low.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated; no direct laboratory analysis or approved database entry exists for this specific dish. Values should be treated as indicative only and updated if a direct source becomes available. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy sources for the component ingredients at the level of detail available. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimation. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for the primary components. 5. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as the dish uses no hydrogenated oils or industrially processed fats. 6. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate from cooked rice noodles (University of Sydney GI Database); the acidic dressing may lower the effective GI slightly below this value. 7. The cuisine tag 'Lor Mee' in the input appears to be a field survey platform tagging error; Rakhine Mont Ti Thoke is not related to Lor Mee (a Chinese-Singaporean braised noodle dish). This tag has been disregarded. 8. Halal status is uncertain as ngapi (fermented fish paste) preparation methods vary; marked is_halal: false as a conservative default. 9. The dish is served at Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant, 77 High Street, Singapore.
